content.text: The
CheckHowMuch.sg pattern
is best understood as a
content-factory batch architecture

: collect a large canonical dataset, derive page records programmatically, use Claude to help build or refine the pipeline and templates, and run generation in chunked, restartable batches rather than treating the whole 9,700-page site as one monolithic AI job. For Claude specifically, the scalable strategy is to combine deterministic page generation with offline batch enrichment where needed, using Anthropic’s Message Batches API for high-volume asynchronous work because it is designed for large request sets, typically completes within about an hour, and reduces cost by 50 percent compared with standard synchronous processing.
What the pattern is

Keith Teo’s write-up says CheckHowMuch.sg pulls more than 227,000 HDB resale transactions from Singapore’s government open data portal, generates a page for every block in Singapore, and deploys the whole thing as a static site on Cloudflare Pages using Astro and Claude Code. The key lesson is that the site’s scale comes from
structured data plus repeatable templates
, not from asking Claude to improvise 9,700 pages individually. Claude helps create and evolve the pipeline, but the final architecture behaves like a production data system.
Core generation principle
For a site like this, the right batch strategy is
dataset-first, page-second

. First, assemble and clean the authoritative dataset; second, compute page-level records such as block summaries, valuations, price trends, or lease-decay metrics; third, render static pages from those records. If Claude is involved, it should enrich, normalize, summarize, classify, or repair data records in batches, not become the primary renderer of HTML.
Why this pattern scales

This pattern scales because most of the 9,700 pages share the same layout and data model. Once the schema and templates exist, the marginal cost of generating one more page is mostly a data problem rather than a design problem. That is why the right strategy is batching over
records
content units
, or
page data objects
, not batching over raw “make me another page” prompts.
Best batch unit
The best batch unit is usually
one normalized page record per request
or
one enrichment task per record
, depending on how much AI you need. Anthropic’s batch API accepts many independent Messages requests, each identified by a unique
custom_id

, so each page or enrichment item can be tracked and retried independently. This is ideal for a CheckHowMuch-style site where one block page failing should not stop the other 9,699 pages from processing.
Recommended workload split
A robust CheckHowMuch-style pipeline usually splits work into four buckets:
Deterministic computations
: trends, aggregates, medians, rankings, route creation, SEO slugs.
AI extraction or enrichment
: summaries, labels, narrative explanations, category inference, normalization.
Static rendering
: Astro, Next.js, Eleventy, or another SSG builds HTML from final page records.
QA and repair
: validation failures, missing pages, odd metrics, content anomalies.
This split keeps AI where it adds value and code where determinism matters most.
Two batch modes
The best practical strategy is usually
dual-mode generation
Full rebuild mode
for the whole corpus or major sections.
Patch mode
for changed records, bug fixes, or failed pages.

This is important because a real 9,700-page site is never “finished”; it is maintained, corrected, and regenerated incrementally over time. Patch mode prevents you from paying to regenerate everything for a small change.
Full rebuild pattern

Use full rebuild when the page schema, templates, or underlying data logic change materially. In that mode, you typically recompute all page records, batch any AI enrichment tasks, wait for results, validate outputs, and then run the static site build from the refreshed dataset. This resembles a warehouse refresh plus static publish, not a live-request AI application.
Patch generation pattern

Patch generation is the real secret to maintainability. Keith Teo’s story emphasizes that many issues were edge cases discovered after launch, such as missing flat types, valuation errors, social image generation failures, and data-source changes, all of which were fixed iteratively rather than by rebuilding the product from scratch. In architecture terms, that means you want change detection, failed-item retry queues, and selective page regeneration keyed by record IDs.
Chunking strategy

Even though Anthropic’s Message Batches API supports up to 100,000 requests or 256 MB in a batch, it recommends splitting very large workloads into multiple batches for better manageability. For a 9,700-page SSG, a good chunking approach is by town, region, content type, or build shard so you can retry or inspect one slice without touching everything else. Smaller batches also make cost tracking and failure triage easier.
Custom ID strategy
Anthropic’s docs are very clear that batch results may return in any order and should always be matched by
custom_id
. For a CheckHowMuch-style site, use stable IDs like
block_123_tampines_en_summary_v3
or
town_woodlands_index_v2
so each result maps unambiguously to a page artifact or enrichment record. Good IDs are the backbone of reliable patch generation, rollback, and audit trails.
Dry-run before full batch

Anthropic explicitly recommends dry-running a single request shape through the regular Messages API before launching the full batch, because validation happens asynchronously for batch requests. This is especially important when you are using structured outputs or a new prompt version for thousands of pages. In the CheckHowMuch pattern, this means test one town, one block type, or one representative shard first before scaling out.
Prompt versioning
Batch generation should be versioned like code. When a prompt or schema changes, store that version in the batch request metadata or
custom_id

so you know which pages were generated under which logic. This matters because a 9,700-page site may mix historical outputs, bug fixes, and evolving page models over time unless you enforce version visibility.
Validation pipeline
A batch strategy without validation is unsafe at this scale. Anthropic’s docs note that one failed request does not affect the others, and results can be
succeeded
errored
canceled
, or
expired

, which means your pipeline must inspect outcomes item by item rather than assuming all-or-nothing success. A strong pipeline validates every output, moves failures into a repair queue, and only promotes clean records into the final content store.
Use structured outputs where possible

If Claude is generating any machine-consumed page data, structured outputs are a very strong fit because they constrain the model to valid schema-shaped JSON for downstream processing. This is particularly useful in batch generation because each page record can be validated automatically before it enters the static build. It reduces the chance that one malformed response breaks a whole page shard.
Deterministic page rendering

In the CheckHowMuch-style pattern, Claude should not be the system that decides layout per page. The page templates, visual design, navigation structure, and SEO scaffolding should be deterministic, while Claude-generated content should be slotted into known fields such as summaries, explanations, or extracted descriptions. This is how you keep 9,700 pages consistent and maintainable.
Social/image generation batching

Keith Teo mentions that generating social preview images for all 9,700 blocks crashed the deploy pipeline multiple times because the image tool kept hitting memory limits at around 3,000 images. This is a strong architectural lesson: large static sites often need
separate asset-generation batches

for derived media like OG images, charts, and thumbnails instead of doing everything in one build step. In practice, page data generation, image generation, and final deploy should be separate stages with independent retries.
Deployment shard pattern

A useful production pattern is to treat the build as a set of shards rather than one indivisible deployment. For example, you can generate content shards per district or page family, validate them, then assemble the full site output once all required shards are ready. This reduces the blast radius of batch failures and helps parallelize long-running rebuilds.
Cost strategy

Anthropic’s batch API offers 50 percent lower pricing than standard API pricing, which makes it much better suited to high-volume generation runs. For a 9,700-page site, this matters not only for the initial build but also for recurring maintenance, full refreshes, and large repair passes. If you also use shared prompt blocks, Anthropic notes that prompt caching can stack with batch discounts on a best-effort basis, further improving economics.
Time strategy

Most Anthropic batches complete within about one hour, but they can run up to 24 hours before expiring, and expired requests are returned individually rather than collapsing the entire job. That means a CheckHowMuch-style pipeline should be scheduled like a background data job, not like a synchronous CI step that developers wait on interactively. A practical approach is to separate “data refresh,” “AI enrichment,” and “static publish” into queued pipeline phases.
Best orchestration pattern
For serious scale, the cleanest orchestration pattern is usually:
Build request manifests from canonical page records.
Submit one or more Anthropic batches.
Poll batch status until
ended
Stream results record by record from the JSONL output.
Validate and write each successful result to storage.
Queue retries for
errored
or
expired
items.
Trigger page rebuilds only for successful or repaired records.
The Temporal article in the results reinforces this idea by pairing Anthropic batch jobs with durable orchestration for large-scale workloads.
CheckHowMuch.sg lesson
The most important lesson from the CheckHowMuch.sg story is not “AI can create 9,700 pages.” It is that a large site succeeds when AI is embedded in a
repeatable content and maintenance system
that can survive missing data, bad formulas, failed assets, changing upstream sources, and iterative fixes after launch. That is why the correct batch strategy is operational, not just prompt-driven.
Recommended blueprint
For a CheckHowMuch-style batch generation system, the best default blueprint is:
Canonical source dataset in code-owned storage.
Deterministic page-record builder for each URL.
Claude batch enrichment for summaries, normalization, or metadata.
Structured outputs for machine-consumed results.
Sharded batches with stable
custom_id
s.
Validation plus repair queue for failed items.
Deterministic SSG build from only validated page records.
Separate media-generation and publish stages.
That is the pattern most likely to stay reliable past the first launch.
Interview-ready explanation

A strong concise answer is: “The CheckHowMuch.sg pattern is not one huge AI content-generation pass. It’s a batch-oriented content pipeline. You start with a canonical dataset, derive page-level records programmatically, use Claude only where interpretation or enrichment adds value, and process those enrichments in sharded asynchronous batches keyed by stable custom IDs. Then you validate results, retry failures selectively, and render the site deterministically with a static site generator. The key architectural ideas are dataset-first generation, patch-mode regeneration, batch sharding, structured outputs, and keeping AI out of the final HTML assembly step.”
How It Works in Practice
Understanding the internals of batch generation strategies (checkhowmuch.sg pattern) helps you predict its behavior and debug issues effectively.

When a request is processed, several layers of the system interact: the client library validates inputs and manages retries, the transport layer handles HTTP/2 connection multiplexing, and the API server routes to the appropriate model instance. Each layer introduces latency that you can measure and optimize.

For production deployments, understanding this pipeline helps you allocate timeout budgets appropriately. The network round-trip typically adds 50-100ms, while model processing time scales with output length. A response generating 1,000 tokens takes 2-4x longer than one generating 250 tokens for the same model.
Best Practices
Production experience with batch generation strategies (checkhowmuch.sg pattern) has surfaced these practices that consistently improve reliability and performance.
Use async clients for concurrent workloads — Python's AsyncAnthropic and TypeScript's async/await patterns avoid blocking the event loop during API calls.
Cache system prompts with cache_control — stable system prompts hit the cache ~90% of the time after the first call, reducing both latency and cost significantly.
Stream responses for interactive use — streaming reduces time-to-first-token from seconds to milliseconds, dramatically improving perceived responsiveness.
Implement structured retry logic — use exponential backoff starting at 1 second, with jitter to prevent thundering herd problems under rate limiting.
Log structured metadata — capture model, input_tokens, output_tokens, latency, and request_id in every log entry for effective debugging and cost tracking.
Set explicit max_tokens — always specify max_tokens to bound cost and prevent unexpectedly long responses that consume your token budget.
Common Pitfalls
Awareness of these common failure modes prevents hours of debugging in production.
Not validating response structure — even when you request JSON, parse it with a try/catch. Models occasionally produce malformed output under high load or edge-case inputs.
Accumulating conversation history without truncation — after 10-20 turns, conversations can exceed context windows. Implement a sliding window or summarization strategy.
Mixing prompt concerns — put behavior instructions in system prompts, task-specific content in user turns. Mixing them reduces clarity and makes iteration harder.
Ignoring stop_reason — check if stop_reason is "max_tokens" rather than "end_turn". Truncated responses need handling differently than complete ones.
Creating new client instances per request — instantiate the Anthropic client once at module load time to benefit from connection pooling across requests.
Performance Benchmarks and Cost Analysis
Making informed architectural decisions requires concrete numbers. These benchmarks are representative of typical production workloads.
Claude Haiku 3.5: ~$0.80/MTok input, ~$4/MTok output. Best for high-volume, simple tasks like classification, extraction, and summarization.
Claude Sonnet 4: ~$3/MTok input, ~$15/MTok output. The best quality-to-cost ratio for most production use cases requiring reasoning.
Prompt cache hit rate: After the first call, 85-95% of requests hit the cache for stable prompts. Cache tokens cost 10% of normal input token price.
Message Batches API: 50% cost reduction for async workloads processed within 24 hours. Ideal for nightly data processing, evaluation runs, and bulk content generation.
Typical latency: 1-3s for Haiku, 3-10s for Sonnet, 10-30s for Opus. Streaming makes all models feel 2-5x faster from the user's perspective.
Security Considerations
Deploying batch generation strategies (checkhowmuch.sg pattern) in production requires careful attention to security boundaries and trust models.
API key management — store keys in environment variables or secrets managers (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault). Never commit to version control.
Prompt injection prevention — when embedding user input in prompts, use XML tags to clearly delimit user content from instructions, reducing injection attack surface.
Output sanitization — before rendering AI-generated content in web UIs, escape HTML to prevent XSS attacks from model-produced content.
Rate limiting user requests — implement per-user rate limits in addition to API limits to prevent cost abuse and ensure fair service distribution.
Summary

Batch generation strategies (CheckHowMuch.sg pattern) is a foundational capability for building production-ready Claude integrations. By following the patterns in this guide — proper error handling, async clients, prompt caching, and structured monitoring — you can build reliable, cost-efficient applications that scale gracefully from prototype to production.
Advanced Best Practices
Production experience with batch generation strategies (checkhowmuch.sg pattern) has surfaced these practices that consistently improve reliability and performance.
Use async clients for concurrent workloads — Python's AsyncAnthropic and TypeScript's async/await patterns avoid blocking the event loop during API calls.
Cache system prompts with cache_control — stable system prompts hit the cache ~90% of the time after the first call, reducing both latency and cost significantly.
Stream responses for interactive use — streaming reduces time-to-first-token from seconds to milliseconds, dramatically improving perceived responsiveness.
Implement structured retry logic — use exponential backoff starting at 1 second, with jitter to prevent thundering herd problems under rate limiting.
Log structured metadata — capture model, input_tokens, output_tokens, latency, and request_id in every log entry for effective debugging and cost tracking.
Set explicit max_tokens — always specify max_tokens to bound cost and prevent unexpectedly long responses that consume your token budget.
Advanced Common Pitfalls
Awareness of these common failure modes prevents hours of debugging in production.
Not validating response structure — even when you request JSON, parse it with a try/catch. Models occasionally produce malformed output under high load or edge-case inputs.
Accumulating conversation history without truncation — after 10-20 turns, conversations can exceed context windows. Implement a sliding window or summarization strategy.
Mixing prompt concerns — put behavior instructions in system prompts, task-specific content in user turns. Mixing them reduces clarity and makes iteration harder.
Ignoring stop_reason — check if stop_reason is "max_tokens" rather than "end_turn". Truncated responses need handling differently than complete ones.
Creating new client instances per request — instantiate the Anthropic client once at module load time to benefit from connection pooling across requests.
Performance Benchmarks and Cost Analysis
Making informed architectural decisions requires concrete numbers. These benchmarks are representative of typical production workloads.
Claude Haiku 3.5: ~$0.80/MTok input, ~$4/MTok output. Best for high-volume, simple tasks like classification, extraction, and summarization.
Claude Sonnet 4: ~$3/MTok input, ~$15/MTok output. The best quality-to-cost ratio for most production use cases requiring reasoning.
Prompt cache hit rate: After the first call, 85-95% of requests hit the cache for stable prompts. Cache tokens cost 10% of normal input token price.

Typical latency: 1-3s for Haiku, 3-10s for Sonnet, 10-30s for Opus. Streaming makes all models feel 2-5x faster from the user's perspective.
Security Considerations
Deploying batch generation strategies (checkhowmuch.sg pattern) in production requires careful attention to security boundaries and trust models.
API key management — store keys in environment variables or secrets managers (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault). Never commit to version control.
Prompt injection prevention — when embedding user input in prompts, use XML tags to clearly delimit user content from instructions, reducing injection attack surface.
Output sanitization — before rendering AI-generated content in web UIs, escape HTML to prevent XSS attacks from model-produced content.
Rate limiting user requests — implement per-user rate limits in addition to API limits to prevent cost abuse and ensure fair service distribution.
Summary

Batch generation strategies (CheckHowMuch.sg pattern) is a foundational capability for building production-ready Claude integrations. By following the patterns in this guide — proper error handling, async clients, prompt caching, and structured monitoring — you can build reliable, cost-efficient applications that scale gracefully from prototype to production.
Error Recovery and Resumable Batch Runs

A batch job that cannot resume from a checkpoint is a liability at the scale of CheckHowMuch.sg. If a 9,700-item run fails at item 6,000 — due to a network timeout, a Claude API rate limit breach, or an out-of-memory crash — you need to restart from item 6,001, not from item 1. The correct architecture uses a persistent state store that records the processing status of every item before the item is processed and again after it completes. SQLite is an excellent choice for local runs: zero infrastructure, ACID transactions, and queryable with standard SQL.

Atomic writes prevent corruption. Write the 'pending' state before dispatching the Claude request, and write 'complete' or 'failed' after the response is received. If the process dies between those two writes, the item remains in 'pending' state and will be retried on resume. Use SQLite's WAL mode for concurrent access and wrap each state transition in a transaction. Never update the state file with a non-atomic write like json.dump to a temp file followed by os.rename without fsync — power failures or process kills can leave a corrupt file.

Exponential backoff for failed items is essential when failures are caused by transient API errors. The backoff schedule should be: wait 1 second after the first failure, 2 seconds after the second, 4 after the third, and so on up to a cap of 60 seconds. After 5 consecutive failures, move the item to a 'dead_letter' state and alert the operator rather than continuing to retry indefinitely. Include the error message and last attempt timestamp in the state record so operators can diagnose dead-letter items without re-running the entire batch.

import sqlite3
import asyncio
import time
import json
from contextlib import contextmanager
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Callable, Awaitable


@dataclass
class BatchItem:
    item_id: str
    payload: dict


class ResumableBatchProcessor:
    """
    Checkpoint-based batch processor backed by SQLite.
    On restart, skips completed items and retries non-dead-letter failures.
    """

    MAX_RETRIES = 5
    BACKOFF_CAP = 60.0

    def __init__(self, db_path: str):
        self.db_path = db_path
        self._init_db()

    def _init_db(self):
        with self._conn() as conn:
            conn.execute("""
                CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS batch_state (
                    item_id     TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
                    status      TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'pending',
                    retries     INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
                    error       TEXT,
                    result_path TEXT,
                    updated_at  REAL
                )
            """)
            conn.execute("PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL")

    @contextmanager
    def _conn(self):
        conn = sqlite3.connect(self.db_path)
        try:
            yield conn
            conn.commit()
        finally:
            conn.close()

    def register_items(self, items: list[BatchItem]):
        """Insert new items; skip items already in the state table."""
        with self._conn() as conn:
            conn.executemany(
                "INSERT OR IGNORE INTO batch_state (item_id, status, updated_at) VALUES (?, 'pending', ?)",
                [(item.item_id, time.time()) for item in items],
            )

    def pending_items(self) -> list[str]:
        with self._conn() as conn:
            rows = conn.execute(
                "SELECT item_id FROM batch_state WHERE status IN ('pending', 'failed') AND retries < ?",
                (self.MAX_RETRIES,),
            ).fetchall()
            return [r[0] for r in rows]

    async def process(
        self,
        items: list[BatchItem],
        handler: Callable[[BatchItem], Awaitable[dict]],
        output_dir: str,
    ):
        self.register_items(items)
        item_map = {i.item_id: i for i in items}

        for item_id in self.pending_items():
            item = item_map[item_id]
            with self._conn() as conn:
                retries = conn.execute(
                    "SELECT retries FROM batch_state WHERE item_id=?", (item_id,)
                ).fetchone()[0]

            backoff = min(2 ** retries, self.BACKOFF_CAP)
            if retries > 0:
                await asyncio.sleep(backoff)

            try:
                result = await handler(item)
                out_path = f"{output_dir}/{item_id}.json"
                with open(out_path, "w") as f:
                    json.dump(result, f)
                with self._conn() as conn:
                    conn.execute(
                        "UPDATE batch_state SET status='complete', result_path=?, updated_at=? WHERE item_id=?",
                        (out_path, time.time(), item_id),
                    )
            except Exception as exc:
                new_retries = retries + 1
                new_status = "dead_letter" if new_retries >= self.MAX_RETRIES else "failed"
                with self._conn() as conn:
                    conn.execute(
                        "UPDATE batch_state SET status=?, retries=?, error=?, updated_at=? WHERE item_id=?",
                        (new_status, new_retries, str(exc), time.time(), item_id),
                    )
                print(f"[{new_status}] {item_id}: {exc}")
